html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Seventeen — Part One

The Book of Yesha'yahu

YESHA'YAHU

The Book of Isaiah — 1QIsa-a Great Isaiah Scroll

Chapters 1 through 12

The Indictment of Yehudah — The Vineyard Song — The Throne Vision — Immanu-El — The Shoot of Yishai

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

1QIsa-a — The Great Isaiah Scroll — Cave 1 Qumran — c. 125-100 BCE

The oldest and most complete prophetic manuscript in existence

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only — No Lord or God substitutions

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 12 — COVENANT JUDGMENT AND THE VISION OF RESTORATION

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 1 The Vision of Yesha'yahu — A Sinful Nation — Come Let Us Reason Together — Tziyon Shall Be Redeemed

1 The vision of Yesha'yahu the son of Amoz, which he saw concerning Yehudah and Yerushalayim in the days of Uzziyahu, Yotam, Akhaz, and Khizkiyahu, kings of Yehudah.

2 Hear, O heavens, and give ear, O earth, for YHWH has spoken: Children have I reared and brought up, but they have rebelled against me.

3 The ox knows its owner, and the donkey its master's crib, but Yisra'EL does not know, my people do not understand.

4 Ah, sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity, offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly. They have forsaken YHWH, they have despised the Holy One of Yisra'EL, they are utterly estranged.

5 Why will you still be struck down? Why will you continue to rebel? The whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint.

6 From the sole of the foot even to the head, there is no soundness in it, but bruises and sores and raw wounds — they are not pressed out or bound up or softened with oil.

7 Your country lies desolate — your cities are burned with fire — in your very presence foreigners devour your land — it is desolate, as overthrown by foreigners.

8 And the daughter of Tziyon is left like a booth in a vineyard, like a lodge in a cucumber field, like a besieged city.

9 If YHWH of hosts had not left us a few survivors, we should have been like Sodom, and become like Gomorrah.

10 Hear the word of YHWH, you rulers of Sodom — give ear to the Torah of our Creator, you people of Gomorrah.

11 What to me is the multitude of your sacrifices? says YHWH. I have had enough of burnt offerings of rams and the fat of well-fed beasts. I do not delight in the blood of bulls, or of lambs, or of goats.

12 When you come to appear before me, who has required of you this trampling of my courts?

13 Bring no more vain offerings — incense is an abomination to me. New moon and Sabbath and the calling of convocations — I cannot endure iniquity and solemn assembly.

14 Your new moons and your appointed feasts my soul hates — they have become a burden to me. I am weary of bearing them.

15 When you spread out your hands, I will hide my eyes from you — even though you make many prayers, I will not listen. Your hands are full of blood.

16 Wash yourselves — make yourselves clean. Remove the evil of your deeds from before my eyes — cease to do evil,

17 Learn to do good — seek justice — correct oppression — bring justice to the fatherless — plead the widow's cause.

18 Come now, let us reason together, says YHWH: though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow — though they are red like crimson, they shall become like wool.

19 If you are willing and obedient, you shall eat the good of the land.

20 But if you refuse and rebel, you shall be eaten by the sword — for the mouth of YHWH has spoken.

21 How the faithful city has become a whore — she who was full of justice. Righteousness lodged in her, but now murderers.

22 Your silver has become dross, your best wine mixed with water.

23 Your princes are rebels and companions of thieves. Everyone loves a bribe and runs after gifts. They do not bring justice to the fatherless, and the widow's cause does not come to them.

24 Therefore the Lord YHWH of hosts, the Mighty One of Yisra'EL, declares: Ah, I will get relief from my enemies and avenge myself on my foes.

25 I will turn my hand against you and will smelt away your dross as with lye and remove all your alloy.

26 And I will restore your judges as at the first, and your counselors as at the beginning. Afterward you shall be called the city of righteousness, the faithful city.

27 Tziyon shall be redeemed by justice, and those in her who repent, by righteousness.

28 But rebels and sinners shall be broken together, and those who forsake YHWH shall be consumed.

29 For they shall be ashamed of the oaks that you desired, and you shall blush for the gardens that you have chosen.

30 For you shall be like an oak whose leaf withers, and like a garden without water.

31 And the strong shall become tinder, and his work a spark, and both of them shall burn together, with none to quench them.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a (the Great Isaiah Scroll, Cave 1, Qumran) preserves the complete text of Yesha'yahu and is the oldest complete prophetic manuscript in existence, dated to approximately 125-100 BCE — roughly one thousand years older than the oldest Masoretic manuscript. Chapter 1 opens the collection with the covenant indictment of Yehudah and Yerushalayim. 1QIsa-a preserves the full text with minor orthographic variants from the Masoretic but no significant textual changes in Chapter 1.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Yesha'yahu in full as part of the Ethiopian canonical tradition. Verse 18 — come now, let us reason together — though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow — is read within the Ge'ez tradition as the foundational covenant offer of restoration through covenant reasoning with YHWH, not through accumulated merit or sacrifice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text and 1QIsa-a are in close agreement throughout Chapter 1. The 1QIsa-a text shows variant spellings and occasional additional words but no substantive doctrinal variants. The Septuagint shows some expansions. This compilation follows 1QIsa-a as the primary witness with Masoretic variants noted where significant.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-15 — YHWH declares that he has had enough of sacrifices, burnt offerings, incense, new moons, feasts, and prayers — because the hands bringing them are full of blood. The covenant diagnosis is surgical: ritual performance disconnected from covenant justice is not merely insufficient — it is actively offensive to YHWH. The solution — verse 16-17 — is not more ritual but covenant ethics: wash, cease evil, learn good, seek justice, correct oppression, defend the fatherless and widow. Verse 18 — the covenant offer of restoration is unconditional in its scope — though your sins are like scarlet — but conditional in its reception: if you are willing and obedient, verse 19. The covenant reasoning of YHWH does not bypass human will.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 2 The Mountain of YHWH — They Shall Beat Their Swords Into Plowshares — The Day of YHWH

1 The word that Yesha'yahu the son of Amoz saw concerning Yehudah and Yerushalayim.

2 It shall come to pass in the latter days that the mountain of the house of YHWH shall be established as the highest of the mountains, and shall be lifted up above the hills, and all the nations shall flow to it,

3 And many peoples shall come, and say: Come, let us go up to the mountain of YHWH, to the house of the Creator of Yaakov, that he may teach us his ways and that we may walk in his paths. For out of Tziyon shall go the Torah, and the word of YHWH from Yerushalayim.

4 He shall judge between the nations, and shall decide disputes for many peoples — and they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks. Nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore.

5 O house of Yaakov, come, let us walk in the light of YHWH.

6 For you have rejected your people, the house of Yaakov, because they are full of things from the east and of fortune-tellers like the Pelishtim, and they strike hands with the children of foreigners.

7 Their land is filled with silver and gold, and there is no end to their treasures. Their land is filled with horses, and there is no end to their chariots.

8 Their land is filled with idols — they bow down to the work of their hands, to what their own fingers have made.

9 So man is humbled, and each one is brought low — do not forgive them.

10 Enter into the rock and hide in the dust from before the terror of YHWH, and from the splendor of his majesty.

11 The haughty looks of man shall be brought low, and the lofty pride of men shall be humbled, and YHWH alone will be exalted in that day.

12 For YHWH of hosts has a day against all that is proud and lofty, against all that is lifted up — and it shall be brought low —

13 Against all the cedars of Lebanon, lofty and lifted up, and against all the oaks of Bashan,

14 Against all the lofty mountains, and against all the uplifted hills,

15 Against every high tower, and against every fortified wall,

16 Against all the ships of Tarshish, and against all the beautiful craft.

17 And the haughtiness of man shall be humbled, and the lofty pride of men shall be brought low, and YHWH alone will be exalted in that day.

18 And the idols shall utterly pass away.

19 And people shall enter the caves of the rocks and the holes of the ground, from before the terror of YHWH, and from the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to terrify the earth.

20 In that day mankind will cast away their idols of silver and their idols of gold, which they made for themselves to worship, to the moles and to the bats,

21 To enter the caverns of the rocks and the clefts of the cliffs, from before the terror of YHWH, and from the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to terrify the earth.

22 Stop regarding man in whose nostrils is breath, for of what account is he?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 2 in full. The vision of the nations streaming to the mountain of YHWH — verses 2-4 — is also found in Mikhah 4:1-3, suggesting an early shared prophetic tradition or mutual citation. 1QIsa-a preserves the text with minor variants. The covenant vision of universal covenant submission to YHWH from Tziyon is one of the most expansive eschatological passages in the entire Nevi'im.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 3 — out of Tziyon shall go the Torah, and the word of YHWH from Yerushalayim — is read within the Ethiopian tradition as the covenant declaration of Yerushalayim's universal covenant centrality: the Torah's origin point is Tziyon, and from there it flows to all nations.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement throughout Chapter 2. Verse 4 — they shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks — is the most widely cited covenant peace declaration in the prophetic corpus, later inverted in Yoel 4:10 — beat your plowshares into swords. Both texts are preserved in 1QIsa-a.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-4 — the latter days vision is the covenant counter-narrative to the present state of Yehudah documented in Chapter 1. The nation that is currently corrupt, rebelling, full of injustice — will become the destination to which all nations stream for covenant instruction. The Torah that Yehudah is currently violating will go out from Tziyon to teach the nations. YHWH's covenant restoration of Yehudah is simultaneously the covenant transformation of the entire world order. Verse 22 — stop regarding man in whose nostrils is breath, for of what account is he? The Day of YHWH argument concludes with the covenant invitation to recalibrate ultimate allegiance: human power — even at its most impressive — is breath in nostrils.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 3 YHWH Removes the Stay and the Staff — The Daughters of Tziyon — Their Finery Replaced With Shame

1 For behold, the Lord YHWH of hosts is taking away from Yerushalayim and from Yehudah support and supply, all support of bread, and all support of water —

2 The mighty man and the soldier, the judge and the prophet, the diviner and the elder,

3 The captain of fifty and the man of rank, the counselor and the skillful magician and the expert in charms.

4 And I will make boys their princes, and infants shall rule over them.

5 And the people will oppress one another — every one his fellow and every one his neighbor. The youth will be insolent to the elder, and the despised to the honorable.

6 For a man will take hold of his brother in the house of his father, saying: You have a cloak, you shall be our leader, and this heap of ruins shall be under your rule.

7 In that day he will speak out, saying: I will not be a healer — in my house there is neither bread nor cloak — you shall not make me leader of the people.

8 For Yerushalayim has stumbled, and Yehudah has fallen, because their speech and their deeds are against YHWH, defying his glorious presence.

9 For the look on their faces bears witness against them — they proclaim their sin like Sodom — they do not hide it. Woe to them. For they have brought evil on themselves.

10 Tell the righteous that it shall be well with them, for they shall eat the fruit of their deeds.

11 Woe to the wicked — it shall be ill with him, for what his hands have dealt out shall be done to him.

12 My people — infants are their oppressors, and women rule over them. O my people, your guides mislead you and they have swallowed up the course of your paths.

13 YHWH has taken his place to contend — he stands to judge peoples.

14 YHWH will enter into judgment with the elders and princes of his people: It is you who have devoured the vineyard, the spoil of the poor is in your houses.

15 What do you mean by crushing my people, by grinding the face of the poor? declares the Lord YHWH of hosts.

16 YHWH said: Because the daughters of Tziyon are haughty and walk with outstretched necks, glancing wantonly with their eyes, mincing along as they go, tinkling with their feet,

17 The Lord will strike with a scab the heads of the daughters of Tziyon, and YHWH will lay bare their secret parts.

18 In that day YHWH will take away the finery of the anklets, the headbands, and the crescents,

19 The pendants, the bracelets, and the scarves,

20 The headdresses, the armlets, the sashes, the perfume boxes, and the amulets,

21 The signet rings and nose rings,

22 The festal robes, the mantles, the cloaks, and the handbags,

23 The mirrors, the linen garments, the turbans, and the veils.

24 Instead of perfume there will be rottenness, and instead of a belt, a rope, and instead of well-set hair, baldness, and instead of a rich robe, a skirt of sackcloth, and branding instead of beauty.

25 Your men shall fall by the sword and your mighty men in battle.

26 And her gates shall lament and mourn, and she shall be desolate and sit on the ground.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 3 in full with minor orthographic variants. The covenant judgment of Yerushalayim's leadership — judges, prophets, elders, counselors all removed — is the covenant consequence of the corruption cataloged in Chapters 1-2. The extensive catalog of feminine finery in verses 18-23 is preserved in full in 1QIsa-a.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 14-15 — it is you who have devoured the vineyard — the spoil of the poor is in your houses — what do you mean by crushing my people, by grinding the face of the poor? — is read as the covenant indictment of economic oppression as covenant violation equivalent to religious apostasy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 3 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 10-11 — tell the righteous it shall be well with them — woe to the wicked it shall be ill with him — is the covenant moral order declared in the midst of social collapse. The covenant principle of corresponding consequence does not disappear even when social structures do.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14-15 — YHWH enters into judgment with the elders and princes. The covenant accusation — you have devoured the vineyard and the spoil of the poor is in your houses — identifies economic exploitation as the primary covenant violation of the ruling class. The crushing of YHWH's people and the grinding of the poor's face are not merely social injustices — they are offenses against YHWH himself. The poor are YHWH's people — verse 15. To grind their face is to grind against YHWH's covenant claim on them.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 4 The Branch of YHWH — The Cloud by Day and Fire by Night — The Remnant of Tziyon Shall Be Holy

1 And seven women shall take hold of one man in that day, saying, We will eat our own bread and wear our own clothes — only let us be called by your name — take away our reproach.

2 In that day the Branch of YHWH shall be beautiful and glorious, and the fruit of the land shall be the pride and honor of the survivors of Yisra'EL.

3 And he who is left in Tziyon and remains in Yerushalayim will be called holy — everyone who has been recorded for life in Yerushalayim,

4 When the Lord shall have washed away the filth of the daughters of Tziyon and cleansed the bloodstains of Yerushalayim from its midst by a spirit of judgment and by a spirit of burning.

5 Then YHWH will create over the whole site of Mount Tziyon and over her assemblies a cloud by day, and smoke and the shining of a flaming fire by night — for over all the glory there will be a canopy.

6 There will be a booth for shade by day from the heat, and for a refuge and a shelter from the storm and rain.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 4 in full. The Branch of YHWH — Tzemakh YHWH — verse 2 — is the earliest occurrence of the Branch messianic title in the prophetic corpus, appearing also in Yirmeyahu 23:5, Zekhariah 3:8 and 6:12. 1QIsa-a preserves this title clearly. The covenant vision of renewed covenant presence — cloud by day and fire by night — deliberately echoes the wilderness theophany of Shemot.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 5-6 — YHWH will create over Mount Tziyon a cloud by day and fire by night — a booth for shade and shelter — is read as the covenant renewal of the wilderness presence: what YHWH provided in the desert for the covenant community will be restored to Tziyon.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 4. The six verse chapter is a covenant bridge between the extended judgment of Chapter 3 and the vineyard song of Chapter 5 — the remnant who survive the judgment become the holy community over whom YHWH's presence returns.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-3 — the Branch of YHWH shall be beautiful and glorious — he who is left in Tziyon will be called holy. The covenant trajectory of Yesha'yahu establishes the pattern: judgment produces a remnant, the remnant is holy, the holy remnant receives the restored covenant presence. The cloud and fire of verse 5 are not mere meteorological phenomena — they are the theophanic presence of YHWH returning to dwell among his purified people. The covenant wilderness experience is the template for the covenant restoration.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 5 The Song of the Vineyard — My Beloved Had a Vineyard — The Six Woes — The Day of YHWH's Anger

1 Let me sing for my beloved my love song concerning his vineyard: My beloved had a vineyard on a very fertile hill.

2 He dug it and cleared it of stones, and planted it with choice vines. He built a watchtower in the midst of it, and hewed out a wine vat in it. And he looked for it to yield grapes, but it yielded wild grapes.

3 And now, O inhabitants of Yerushalayim and men of Yehudah, judge between me and my vineyard.

4 What more was there to do for my vineyard, that I have not done in it? When I looked for it to yield grapes, why did it yield wild grapes?

5 And now I will tell you what I will do to my vineyard. I will remove its hedge, and it shall be devoured. I will break down its wall, and it shall be trampled down.

6 I will make it a waste — it shall not be pruned or hoed, and briers and thorns shall grow up. I will also command the clouds that they rain no rain upon it.

7 For the vineyard of YHWH of hosts is the house of Yisra'EL, and the men of Yehudah are his pleasant planting. And he looked for justice, but behold, bloodshed — for righteousness, but behold, an outcry.

8 Woe to those who join house to house, who add field to field, until there is no more room, and you are made to dwell alone in the midst of the land.

9 YHWH of hosts has sworn in my hearing: Surely many houses shall be desolate, large and beautiful houses, without inhabitant.

10 For ten acres of vineyard shall yield but one bath, and a homer of seed shall yield but an ephah.

11 Woe to those who rise early in the morning, that they may run after strong drink, who tarry late into the evening as wine inflames them.

12 They have lyre and harp, tambourine and flute and wine at their feasts, but they do not regard the deeds of YHWH, or see the work of his hands.

13 Therefore my people go into exile for lack of knowledge. And their honored men go hungry, and their multitude is parched with thirst.

14 Therefore Sheol has enlarged its appetite and opened its mouth beyond measure, and the nobility of Yerushalayim and her multitude will go down, her revelers and he who exults in her.

15 Man is humbled, and each one is brought low, and the eyes of the haughty are brought low.

16 But YHWH of hosts is exalted in justice, and the Holy Creator shows himself holy in righteousness.

17 Then the lambs shall graze as in their pasture, and nomads shall eat among the ruins of the rich.

18 Woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood, who draw sin as with cart ropes,

19 Who say: Let him be quick, let him speed his work that we may see it. Let the counsel of the Holy One of Yisra'EL draw near, and let it come, that we may know it.

20 Woe to those who call evil good and good evil, who put darkness for light and light for darkness, who put bitter for sweet and sweet for bitter.

21 Woe to those who are wise in their own eyes, and shrewd in their own sight.

22 Woe to those who are heroes at drinking wine, and valiant men in mixing strong drink,

23 Who acquit the guilty for a bribe, and deprive the innocent of his right.

24 Therefore, as the tongue of fire devours the stubble, and as dry grass sinks down in the flame, so their root will be as rottenness, and their blossom go up like dust — for they have rejected the Torah of YHWH of hosts, and have despised the word of the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

25 Therefore the anger of YHWH was kindled against his people, and he stretched out his hand against them and struck them, and the mountains quaked, and their corpses were as refuse in the midst of the streets. For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is stretched out still.

26 He will raise a signal for nations far away, and whistle for them from the ends of the earth. And behold, quickly, speedily they come.

27 None is weary, none stumbles, none slumbers or sleeps, not a waistband is loose, not a sandal strap broken.

28 Their arrows are sharp, all their bows bent, their horses' hoofs seem like flint, and their wheels like the whirlwind.

29 Their roaring is like a lion, like young lions they roar. They growl and seize their prey — they carry it off, and none can rescue.

30 And they will growl over it on that day, like the growling of the sea. And if one looks to the land, behold, darkness and distress — and the light is darkened by its clouds.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 5 in full with minor variants. The Song of the Vineyard — verses 1-7 — is the most sustained covenant parable in Yesha'yahu, deploying the image of YHWH as the vineyard owner who did everything possible for his planting and received only wild grapes. The parable structure — heard as a love song before the application is revealed — is a covenant rhetorical device paralleling Natan's parable to Dawid.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. The six woes — verses 8, 11, 18, 20, 21, 22 — are read as the covenant catalog of Yehudah's specific violations: land accumulation, drunkenness, moral inversion, self-wisdom, and judicial corruption. Each woe is a specific form of covenant blindness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 5 in close agreement with the Masoretic. Verse 7 — he looked for justice but behold, bloodshed — for righteousness but behold, an outcry — deploys a Hebrew wordplay: mishpat/mispakh (justice/bloodshed) and tzedakah/tze'akah (righteousness/outcry). The wordplay is preserved in 1QIsa-a.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 20 — woe to those who call evil good and good evil, who put darkness for light and light for darkness, who put bitter for sweet and sweet for bitter. This is not merely moral confusion but covenant inversion — a deliberate reversal of the covenant order that YHWH established. The sixth woe is the most comprehensive: it describes a community that has not merely violated specific covenant commands but has systematically inverted the covenant categories of good and evil themselves. Verse 24 — they have rejected the Torah of YHWH of hosts and despised the word of the Holy One of Yisra'EL. The root cause beneath all six woes.

THE THRONE VISION AND THE IMMANUEL SIGN — CHAPTERS 6 THROUGH 12

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 6 The Throne Vision — Holy Holy Holy — Here I Am Send Me — Go and Make Their Hearts Dull

1 In the year that King Uzziyahu died I saw YHWH sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up, and the train of his robe filled the temple.

2 Above him stood the serafim. Each had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew.

3 And one called to another and said: Holy, holy, holy is YHWH of hosts — the whole earth is full of his glory.

4 And the foundations of the thresholds shook at the voice of him who called, and the house was filled with smoke.

5 And I said: Woe is me. For I am lost, for I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips — for my eyes have seen the King, YHWH of hosts.

6 Then one of the serafim flew to me, having in his hand a burning coal that he had taken with tongs from the altar.

7 And he touched my mouth and said: Behold, this has touched your lips. Your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for.

8 And I heard the voice of YHWH saying, Whom shall I send, and who will go for us? Then I said, Here I am — send me.

9 And he said, Go, and say to this people: Keep on hearing, but do not understand — keep on seeing, but do not perceive.

10 Make the heart of this people dull, and their ears heavy, and blind their eyes — lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their hearts, and turn and be healed.

11 Then I said, How long, O YHWH? And he said: Until cities lie waste without inhabitant, and houses without people, and the land is a desolate waste,

12 And YHWH removes people far away, and the forsaken places are many in the midst of the land.

13 And though a tenth remain in it, it will be burned again, like a terebinth or an oak, whose stump remains when it is felled. The holy seed is its stump.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 6 in full. The throne vision of Yesha'yahu — the trishagion — holy holy holy is YHWH of hosts — is one of the most theologically dense passages in the entire prophetic canon. The serafim are mentioned only here in the Hebrew canon. 1QIsa-a spells serafim consistently and preserves the full throne vision text.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 3 — holy holy holy is YHWH of hosts — the whole earth is full of his glory — is the covenant declaration of divine transcendence and immanence held simultaneously: YHWH is wholly other — three times holy — and simultaneously present throughout the whole earth in his glory.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 6. Verse 13 — the holy seed is its stump — is the covenant seed of hope at the end of the judgment declaration. The tree is felled. The stump remains. The holy seed within the stump is the remnant theology that will govern the entire second half of Yesha'yahu.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — woe is me, for I am lost, for I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips — for my eyes have seen the King, YHWH of hosts. The covenant prophet's first response to the throne vision is not exaltation but self-annihilation. The holiness of YHWH makes the prophet's uncleanness visible. The response is not pride at being chosen but recognition of covenant disqualification. Verse 8 — here I am, send me. After the coal touches the lips and the covenant disqualification is addressed, the volunteer response is immediate. Verse 9-10 — the commission is paradoxical: go and preach in such a way that the people will not understand. The hardening is not YHWH's desire but YHWH's covenant response to a people who have already chosen not to hear.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 7 The Syro-Ephraimite Crisis — Ask a Sign From YHWH — Behold the Young Woman — Immanuel

1 In the days of Akhaz the son of Yotam, son of Uzziyahu, king of Yehudah, Retzin the king of Aram and Pekakh the son of Remaliyahu the king of Yisra'EL came up to Yerushalayim to wage war against it, but could not yet mount an attack against it.

2 When the house of Dawid was told, Aram is in league with Efrayim, the heart of Akhaz and the heart of his people shook as the trees of the forest shake before the wind.

3 And YHWH said to Yesha'yahu, Go out to meet Akhaz, you and Shear-Yashuv your son, at the end of the conduit of the upper pool on the highway to the Washer's Field.

4 And say to him, Be careful, be quiet, do not fear, and do not let your heart be faint because of these two smoldering stumps of firebrands — at the fierce anger of Retzin and Aram and the son of Remaliyahu.

5 Because Aram, with Efrayim and the son of Remaliyahu, has devised evil against you, saying,

6 Let us go up against Yehudah and terrify it, and let us conquer it for ourselves, and set up the son of Tave'el as king in the midst of it —

7 Thus says the Lord YHWH: It shall not stand, and it shall not come to pass.

8 For the head of Aram is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Retzin. And within sixty-five years Efrayim will be shattered from being a people.

9 And the head of Efrayim is Samaria, and the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliyahu. If you are not firm in faith, you will not be firm at all.

10 Again YHWH spoke to Akhaz:

11 Ask a sign of YHWH your Creator. Let it be deep as Sheol or high as heaven.

12 But Akhaz said, I will not ask, and I will not put YHWH to the test.

13 And he said, Hear then, O house of Dawid. Is it too little for you to weary men, that you weary my Creator also?

14 Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign. Behold, the young woman shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanu-El.

15 He shall eat curds and honey when he knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good.

16 For before the boy knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good, the land whose two kings you dread will be deserted.

17 YHWH will bring upon you and upon your people and upon your father's house such days as have not come since the day that Efrayim departed from Yehudah — the king of Assyria.

18 In that day YHWH will whistle for the fly that is at the sources of the streams of Egypt, and for the bee that is in the land of Assyria.

19 And they will all come and settle in the steep ravines, and in the clefts of the rocks, and on all the thornbushes, and on all the pastures.

20 In that day YHWH will shave with a razor that is hired beyond the River — with the king of Assyria — the head and the hair of the feet, and it will sweep away the beard also.

21 In that day a man will keep alive a young cow and two sheep,

22 And because of the abundance of milk that they give, he will eat curds, for everyone who is left in the land will eat curds and honey.

23 In that day every place where there used to be a thousand vines, worth a thousand shekels of silver, will become briers and thorns.

24 With bow and arrows a man will come there, for all the land will be briers and thorns.

25 And as for all the hills that used to be hoed with a hoe, you will not come there for fear of briers and thorns, but they will become a place where cattle are let loose and where sheep tread.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 14 — behold, the young woman shall conceive and bear a son and shall call his name Immanu-El — is one of the most significant textual points in 1QIsa-a. The Hebrew word used is almah — young woman of marriageable age — not betulah (virgin). 1QIsa-a preserves almah. The Septuagint rendered it parthenos (virgin). This compilation follows 1QIsa-a and the Hebrew text: almah.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. The covenant sign of Immanu-El — Creator with us — is read within the Ge'ez tradition in its immediate historical context: a sign to Akhaz concerning the two kings threatening Yehudah, whose lands will be desolate before the child knows to choose good over evil.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 7 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 9 — if you are not firm in faith, you will not be firm at all — is a Hebrew wordplay: im lo ta'aminu ki lo te'amenu. The covenant condition of political stability is covenant faithfulness — the same word root. Stability flows from faith in YHWH's covenant word, not from political alliances with Assyria.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-12 — ask a sign of YHWH — Akhaz said, I will not ask and I will not put YHWH to the test. Akhaz's refusal is piety language masking covenant rejection. He has already decided to appeal to Assyria — Chapter 16:7 of Melakhim Bet — and cannot afford to receive a covenant sign that would obligate him to YHWH rather than Tiglath-Pileser. Verse 14 — therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign. Because Akhaz refused to ask, YHWH gives the sign anyway. The covenant sign is not for Akhaz alone — verse 13 makes clear it is addressed to the whole house of Dawid. The Immanu-El sign — Creator with us — functions both as immediate historical confirmation and as the covenant declaration that YHWH's presence with his people cannot be eliminated by any political decision a Davidic king makes.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 8 Maher-Shalal-Khash-Baz — The Waters of Shiloakh — Do Not Fear What They Fear — YHWH of Hosts

1 Then YHWH said to me, Take a large tablet and write on it in common characters, Maher-Shalal-Khash-Baz.

2 And I will get reliable witnesses, Uriyah the priest and Zekhariah the son of Yeverekh'yahu, to attest for me.

3 And I went to the prophetess, and she conceived and bore a son. Then YHWH said to me, Call his name Maher-Shalal-Khash-Baz.

4 For before the boy knows how to cry Father or Mother, the wealth of Damascus and the spoil of Samaria will be carried away before the king of Assyria.

5 YHWH spoke to me again:

6 Because this people has refused the waters of Shiloakh that flow gently, and rejoice over Retzin and the son of Remaliyahu,

7 Therefore, behold, YHWH is bringing up against them the waters of the River — mighty and many — the king of Assyria and all his glory. And it will rise over all its channels and go over all its banks,

8 And it will sweep on into Yehudah, it will overflow and pass on, reaching even to the neck, and its outspread wings will fill the breadth of your land, O Immanu-El.

9 Be broken, you peoples, and be shattered — give ear, all you far countries. Strap on your armor and be shattered — strap on your armor and be shattered.

10 Take counsel together, but it will come to nothing — speak a word, but it will not stand, for Creator is with us.

11 For YHWH spoke thus to me with his strong hand upon me, and warned me not to walk in the way of this people, saying,

12 Do not call conspiracy all that this people calls conspiracy, and do not fear what they fear, nor be in dread.

13 But YHWH of hosts, him you shall honor as holy. Let him be your fear, and let him be your dread.

14 And he will become a sanctuary and a stone of offense and a rock of stumbling to both houses of Yisra'EL, a trap and a snare to the inhabitants of Yerushalayim.

15 And many shall stumble on it. They shall fall and be broken — they shall be snared and taken.

16 Bind up the testimony — seal the Torah among my disciples.

17 I will wait for YHWH, who is hiding his face from the house of Yaakov, and I will hope in him.

18 Behold, I and the children whom YHWH has given me are signs and portents in Yisra'EL from YHWH of hosts, who dwells on Mount Tziyon.

19 And when they say to you, Inquire of the mediums and the necromancers who chirp and mutter — should not a people inquire of their Creator? Should they inquire of the dead on behalf of the living?

20 To the Torah and to the testimony. If they will not speak according to this word, it is because they have no dawn.

21 They will pass through the land, greatly distressed and hungry. And when they are hungry, they will be enraged and will speak contemptuously against their king and their Creator, and turn their faces upward.

22 And they will look to the earth, but behold, distress and darkness, the gloom of anguish. And they will be thrust into thick darkness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 8 in full. The prophet's own children are covenant signs — Shear-Yashuv (a remnant shall return) and Maher-Shalal-Khash-Baz (hasten the spoil, hurry the prey). The covenant theology of prophetic children as living covenant declarations is unique to Yesha'yahu in the Nevi'im.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 20 — to the Torah and to the testimony — if they will not speak according to this word, it is because they have no dawn — is read as the covenant epistemological standard: the Torah and the covenant testimony are the measuring rod by which all prophetic claims are evaluated.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 8 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 13-14 — YHWH of hosts, him you shall honor as holy — let him be your fear — and he will become a sanctuary and a stone of offense. The same YHWH who is the sanctuary for those who fear him is the stone of stumbling for those who do not. The covenant reality is dual-edged depending on the covenantal orientation of the one who encounters it.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12-13 — do not fear what they fear, nor be in dread. But YHWH of hosts, him you shall honor as holy — let him be your fear. The covenant alternative to political fear is not courage but a different fear entirely: the fear of YHWH rather than the fear of kings and armies. The political crisis that drives Akhav to Assyria and drives the people to mediums — verse 19 — is answered not by political wisdom but by covenant reorientation. What you fear determines what you seek for help. If you fear YHWH, you seek YHWH. If you fear Assyria, you seek Assyria.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 9 The People Who Walked in Darkness — A Great Light — Unto Us a Child Is Born — The Zeal of YHWH

1 But there will be no gloom for her who was in anguish. In the former time he brought into contempt the land of Zevulun and the land of Naftali, but in the latter time he has made glorious the way of the sea, the land beyond the Yarden, Galil of the nations.

2 The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light — those who dwelt in a land of deep darkness, on them has light shone.

3 You have multiplied the nation — you have increased its joy. They rejoice before you as with joy at the harvest, as they are glad when they divide the spoil.

4 For the yoke of his burden, and the staff for his shoulder, and the rod of his oppressor, you have broken as on the day of Midyan.

5 For every boot of the tramping warrior in battle tumult and every garment rolled in blood will be burned as fuel for the fire.

6 For to us a child is born, to us a son is given — and the government shall be upon his shoulder — and his name shall be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty Creator, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.

7 Of the increase of his government and of peace there will be no end, on the throne of Dawid and over his kingdom, to establish it and to uphold it with justice and with righteousness from this time forth and forevermore. The zeal of YHWH of hosts will do this.

8 YHWH has sent a word against Yaakov, and it will fall on Yisra'EL.

9 And all the people will know, Efrayim and the inhabitants of Samaria, who say in pride and in arrogance of heart:

10 The bricks have fallen, but we will build with dressed stones — the sycamores have been cut down, but we will put cedars in their place.

11 But YHWH raises the adversaries of Retzin against him, and stirs up his enemies.

12 The Arameans on the east and the Pelishtim on the west devour Yisra'EL with open mouth. For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is stretched out still.

13 The people did not turn to him who struck them, nor inquire of YHWH of hosts.

14 So YHWH cut off from Yisra'EL head and tail, palm branch and reed in one day —

15 The elder and honored man is the head, and the prophet who teaches lies is the tail.

16 For those who guide this people have been leading them astray, and those who are guided by them are swallowed up.

17 Therefore YHWH does not rejoice over their young men, and has no compassion on their fatherless and widows — for everyone is godless and an evildoer, and every mouth speaks folly. For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is stretched out still.

18 For wickedness burns like a fire — it consumes briers and thorns — it kindles the thickets of the forest, and they roll upward in a column of smoke.

19 Through the wrath of YHWH of hosts the land is scorched, and the people are like fuel for the fire. No one spares his brother.

20 They slice meat on the right, but are still hungry, and they devour on the left, but are not satisfied. Each devours the flesh of his own arm —

21 Menasheh devours Efrayim, and Efrayim devours Menasheh — together they are against Yehudah. For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is stretched out still.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 6 — for to us a child is born, to us a son is given — and the government shall be upon his shoulder — and his name shall be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty Creator, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace — is one of the most debated verses in the scroll. 1QIsa-a preserves the Hebrew text essentially as the Masoretic. The four throne names describe the character and authority of the covenant king.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. The fourfold name of verse 6 — Wonderful Counselor, Mighty Creator, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace — is read in the Ethiopian tradition as a covenant declaration of the qualities of the ideal Davidic king who will fulfill the covenant promises made to Dawid in 2 Shemu'el 7.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 9 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The refrain — for all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is stretched out still — appears four times in Chapters 9-10, forming the covenant structure of the judgment section: each covenant failure answered by YHWH's continued judgment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6-7 — of the increase of his government and of peace there will be no end, on the throne of Dawid — to establish it and to uphold it with justice and with righteousness from this time forth and forevermore. The zeal of YHWH of hosts will do this. The covenant declaration that the Davidic throne will be established in justice and righteousness forever is grounded entirely in YHWH's zeal — not in human covenant faithfulness. The kingdom that human kings failed to establish through covenant obedience will be established by YHWH's own covenant passion. Verse 2 — the people who walked in darkness have seen a great light. The covenant geography of the promise — Zevulun and Naftali, the land of Galil — is the region of the northern exile, the first to fall to Assyria.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 10 Woe to the Unjust Legislators — Assyria the Rod of YHWH's Anger — A Remnant Shall Return — The Stump of Yishai

1 Woe to those who decree iniquitous decrees, and the writers who keep writing oppression,

2 To turn aside the needy from justice and to rob the poor of my people of their right, that widows may be their spoil, and that they may make the fatherless their prey.

3 What will you do on the day of punishment, in the ruin that will come from afar? To whom will you flee for help, and where will you leave your wealth?

4 Nothing remains but to crouch among the prisoners or fall among the slain. For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is stretched out still.

5 Woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger — the staff in their hands is my fury.

6 Against a godless nation I send him, and against the people of my wrath I command him, to take spoil and seize plunder, and to tread them down like the mire of the streets.

7 But he does not so intend, and his heart does not so think — but it is in his heart to destroy, and to cut off nations not a few.

8 For he says: Are not my commanders all kings?

9 Is not Kalno like Carchemish? Is not Khamath like Arpad? Is not Samaria like Damascus?

10 As my hand has reached to the kingdoms of the idols, whose carved images were greater than those of Yerushalayim and Samaria —

11 Shall I not do to Yerushalayim and her idols as I have done to Samaria and her images?

12 When the Lord has finished all his work on Mount Tziyon and on Yerushalayim, he will punish the speech of the arrogant heart of the king of Assyria and the boastful look of his eyes.

13 For he says: By the strength of my hand I have done it, and by my wisdom, for I have understanding. I remove the boundaries of peoples, and plunder their treasures. Like a bull I bring down those who sit on thrones.

14 My hand has found like a nest the wealth of the peoples, and as one gathers eggs that have been forsaken, so I have gathered all the earth, and there was none that moved a wing or opened the mouth or chirped.

15 Shall the axe boast over him who hews with it, or the saw magnify itself against him who wields it? As if a rod should wield him who lifts it, or as if a staff should lift him who is not wood.

16 Therefore the Lord YHWH of hosts will send wasting sickness among his stout warriors, and under his glory a burning will be kindled, like the burning of fire.

17 The light of Yisra'EL will become a fire, and his Holy One a flame, and it will burn and devour his thorns and briers in one day.

18 The glory of his forest and of his fruitful land YHWH will destroy, both soul and body, and it will be as when a sick man wastes away.

19 The remnant of the trees of his forest will be so few that a child can write them down.

20 In that day the remnant of Yisra'EL and the survivors of the house of Yaakov will no more lean on him who struck them, but will lean on YHWH, the Holy One of Yisra'EL, in truth.

21 A remnant will return, the remnant of Yaakov, to the Mighty Creator.

22 For though your people Yisra'EL be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will return. Destruction is decreed, overflowing with righteousness.

23 For the Lord YHWH of hosts will make a full end, as decreed, in the midst of all the earth.

24 Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH of hosts: O my people, who dwell in Tziyon, be not afraid of the Assyrians when they strike with the rod and lift up their staff against you as Egypt did.

25 For in a very little while my fury will come to an end, and my anger will be directed to their destruction.

26 And YHWH of hosts will wield against them a whip, as when he struck Midyan at the rock of Orev. And his staff will be over the sea, and he will lift it as he did in Egypt.

27 And in that day his burden will depart from your shoulder, and his yoke from your neck, and the yoke will be broken because of the fat.

28 He has come to Ayat, he has passed through Migron, at Mikhmas he stores his baggage.

29 They have crossed over the pass — at Geva they lodge for the night. Ramah trembles, Giv'ah of Sha'ul has fled.

30 Cry out, O daughter of Gallim — give attention, O Layshah — O poor Anathoth.

31 Madmenah is in flight, the inhabitants of Gevim flee for safety.

32 This very day he will halt at Nov, shaking his fist at the mount of the daughter of Tziyon, the hill of Yerushalayim.

33 Behold, the Lord YHWH of hosts will lop the boughs with terrifying power. The great in height will be hewn down, and the lofty will be brought low.

34 He will cut down the thickets of the forest with an axe, and Lebanon will fall by the Majestic One.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verses 5-15 — the Assyrian rod oracle — is one of the most sophisticated covenant theological arguments in the prophetic corpus: YHWH uses Assyria as his instrument of judgment against Yisra'EL, then judges Assyria for the arrogance it displayed in carrying out that role. The instrument of YHWH's judgment is not exempt from YHWH's judgment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verses 20-21 — a remnant will return, the remnant of Yaakov, to the Mighty Creator — is the covenant declaration that gives Yesha'yahu's son his name: Shear-Yashuv — a remnant shall return. The covenant remnant theology is the thread connecting the judgment sections of Yesha'yahu to the restoration sections.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 10. Verse 15 — shall the axe boast over him who hews with it? — is the covenant logic that limits all human imperial ambition: the tool cannot claim the craftsman's achievement. Assyria's victories were YHWH's acts. Assyria's arrogance in claiming them as its own is the ground of its own judgment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-7 — woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger — I send him against a godless nation — but he does not so intend, and his heart does not so think — but it is in his heart to destroy. The covenant theological paradox: YHWH's intent and Assyria's intent are entirely different. YHWH uses Assyria's destructive ambition as a covenant instrument while Assyria pursues its own imperialist agenda. YHWH holds Assyria accountable for its intentions — verse 12 — even while using the results for covenant purposes. Verse 21 — a remnant will return to the Mighty Creator — El Gibbor — the same title applied to the promised child in verse 9:6. The remnant returns to the one whose character was declared in the throne names.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 11 A Shoot From the Stump of Yishai — The Spirit of YHWH Upon Him — The Wolf and the Lamb — The Root of Yishai

1 There shall come forth a shoot from the stump of Yishai, and a branch from his roots shall bear fruit.

2 And the Spirit of YHWH shall rest upon him, the spirit of wisdom and understanding, the spirit of counsel and might, the spirit of knowledge and the fear of YHWH.

3 And his delight shall be in the fear of YHWH. He shall not judge by what his eyes see, or decide disputes by what his ears hear,

4 But with righteousness he shall judge the poor, and decide with equity for the meek of the earth. And he shall strike the earth with the rod of his mouth, and with the breath of his lips he shall kill the wicked.

5 Righteousness shall be the belt of his waist, and faithfulness the belt of his loins.

6 The wolf shall dwell with the lamb, and the leopard shall lie down with the young goat, and the calf and the lion and the fattened calf together — and a little child shall lead them.

7 The cow and the bear shall graze — their young shall lie down together — and the lion shall eat straw like the ox.

8 The nursing child shall play over the hole of the cobra, and the weaned child shall put his hand on the adder's den.

9 They shall not hurt or destroy in all my holy mountain, for the earth shall be full of the knowledge of YHWH as the waters cover the sea.

10 In that day the root of Yishai, who shall stand as a signal for the peoples — of him shall the nations inquire, and his resting place shall be glorious.

11 In that day the Lord will extend his hand yet a second time to recover the remnant that remains of his people, from Assyria, from Egypt, from Pathros, from Kush, from Elam, from Shinar, from Khamath, and from the coastlands of the sea.

12 He will raise a signal for the nations and will assemble the banished of Yisra'EL, and gather the dispersed of Yehudah from the four corners of the earth.

13 The jealousy of Efrayim shall depart, and those who harass Yehudah shall be cut off. Efrayim shall not be jealous of Yehudah, and Yehudah shall not harass Efrayim.

14 But they shall swoop down on the shoulder of the Pelishtim in the west, and together they shall plunder the people of the east. They shall put out their hand against Edom and Moav, and the Ammonites shall obey them.

15 And YHWH will utterly destroy the tongue of the Sea of Egypt, and will wave his hand over the River with his scorching breath, and strike it into seven channels, and he will lead people across in sandals.

16 And there will be a highway from Assyria for the remnant that remains of his people, as there was for Yisra'EL when they came up from the land of Egypt.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 11 in full. The shoot from the stump of Yishai — verse 1 — continues the covenant remnant theology of Chapter 10:33-34: the great trees of Assyria and Lebanon are felled, but from the cut stump of Yishai's line a new shoot emerges. 1QIsa-a preserves the sevenfold Spirit enumeration of verse 2 clearly.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full. Verses 6-9 — the wolf dwelling with the lamb, the earth full of the knowledge of YHWH — is read in the Ethiopian tradition as the covenant vision of creation restored to its original covenant harmony: the violence introduced by the covenant rupture of Bereshit reversed by the covenant king whose rod strikes the earth and whose breath kills the wicked.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 11 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 10 — the root of Yishai who shall stand as a signal for the peoples — of him shall the nations inquire — connects the Davidic covenant to universal covenant reach. The covenant king is not the exclusive property of Yisra'EL but the covenant destination for all nations.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-3 — the Spirit of YHWH shall rest upon him — wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, and the fear of YHWH — and his delight shall be in the fear of YHWH. The seven-fold Spirit enumeration is the covenant equipping of the ideal Davidic king. The critical covenant detail is verse 3: the king does not judge by what his eyes see or decide by what his ears hear. The covenant judicial standard is not empirical observation but covenant discernment — the fear of YHWH. Verse 9 — the earth shall be full of the knowledge of YHWH as the waters cover the sea. The covenant vision is not mere religious improvement but total saturation of creation with the knowledge of YHWH.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 12 The Song of Salvation — YHWH Is My Strength and My Song — Draw Water From the Wells of Salvation

1 You will say in that day: I will give thanks to you, O YHWH, for though you were angry with me, your anger turned away, that you might comfort me.

2 Behold, Creator is my salvation — I will trust, and will not be afraid, for YHWH Creator is my strength and my song, and he has become my salvation.

3 With joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation.

4 And you will say in that day: Give thanks to YHWH, call upon his name, make known his deeds among the peoples, proclaim that his name is exalted.

5 Sing praises to YHWH, for he has done gloriously — let this be made known in all the earth.

6 Shout, and sing for joy, O inhabitant of Tziyon, for great in your midst is the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 12 in full. The brief thanksgiving psalm that closes the first major section of Yesha'yahu — Chapters 1-12 — serves as the covenant response to the judgment and restoration promises that precede it. The six verses function as a covenant doxology: the restoration promised in Chapters 9-11 produces the thanksgiving of Chapter 12.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. Verse 2 — behold, Creator is my salvation — I will trust and will not be afraid — for YHWH Creator is my strength and my song, and he has become my salvation — is a direct citation and expansion of Shemot 15:2, the Song of the Sea. The covenant salvation at the Exodus is the template for the covenant restoration Yesha'yahu promises.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 12. Verse 3 — with joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation — is the covenant image that will appear in the later prophetic tradition as the image of freely available salvation. The wells of salvation are not exclusive covenant property but accessible with joy.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4-6 — make known his deeds among the peoples, proclaim that his name is exalted — shout and sing for joy, O inhabitant of Tziyon, for great in your midst is the Holy One of Yisra'EL. The covenant response to YHWH's salvation is not private gratitude but public proclamation to the nations. The covenant restoration of Tziyon has a universal covenant purpose: the nations must hear what YHWH has done. The Holy One of Yisra'EL — a title unique to Yesha'yahu in the Nevi'im, appearing 26 times — is the covenant name that identifies the God of Yisra'EL as the Absolute Holy One whose holiness defines the covenant standard.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 12

Volume Seventeen — Yesha'yahu — Part One

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

CONTINUES IN PART TWO — CHAPTERS 13 THROUGH 27
